report_name: EU Digital Sovereignty at Risk as Supply Chain Attacks on European Businesses Escalate

attack_patterns.name: Trusted Relationship
attack_patterns.description: Attackers are targeting third-party providers as an indirect route to compromise their ultimate targets, bypassing the stronger perimeter defenses of the final target by exploiting weaker security at a smaller, trusted vendor.

attack_patterns.name: Supply Chain Compromise
attack_patterns.description: The report highlights the critical and growing risk to European businesses from supply chain cyberattacks, exemplified by incidents at Collins Aerospace and Synnovis, where compromising a single third-party supplier caused widespread disruption.

identities.name: Collins Aerospace
identities.description: A company whose MUSE check-in software was attacked in September 2025, causing significant disruptions at major European airports.

identities.name: Synnovis
identities.description: A UK pathology services supplier and a critical part of the NHS supply chain that suffered a ransomware attack in June 2024, leading to the cancellation of over 800 hospital operations.

identities.name: National Health Service
identities.description: The UK's healthcare system, which was severely impacted by the ransomware attack on its supply chain partner, Synnovis.

identities.name: Major European Airports
identities.description: A class of victims, including airports in London, Brussels, and Berlin, that experienced significant disruptions due to the attack on Collins Aerospace's check-in software.
